phenomenon: perceptual inference from static representation
claim: inferring motion from static depiction engages the same perceptual circuits as observing real motion.

phenomenon: attentional priority for threat-relevant stimuli
claim: young children share enhanced detection of evolutionarily salient threat stimuli with adults, revealing early-emerging adaptive attention mechanisms.

phenomenon: influence of spatial configuration on judgment and affect
claim: perceptual and motor experience of spatial distance influences thoughts and feelings independently of self-reference, expanding distance effects beyond self-centered construal.

phenomenon: validity of cross-national personality assessment
claim: self- and peer-report measures of national personality differences are invalid due to reference-group effects that contaminate aggregated scores.

phenomenon: goal-driven emotion regulation and experience
claim: individuals regulate emotions to serve instrumental goals, choosing potentially aversive states when functionally adaptive despite immediate hedonic costs.

phenomenon: valuation of prediction accuracy versus optimistic bias
claim: optimistic bias in personal predictions reflects alignment with people's values rather than irrationality, if they believe unrealism has instrumental worth.

phenomenon: hedonic valuation of fair interpersonal exchange
claim: fairness independently generates hedonic reward beyond monetary payoff; rejecting unfair proposals engages emotion suppression mechanisms.

phenomenon: gender differences in decoding ambiguous nonverbal intent
claim: gender differences in misinterpreting nonverbal cues reflect male perceptual insensitivity to interpersonal nuance rather than lenient decision thresholds.

phenomenon: mapping personality traits to spatial attention priorities
claim: dominant individuals preferentially attend to the vertical dimension of space, reflecting metaphoric representation of hierarchy in online cognition.

phenomenon: constituents of hedonic well-being
claim: happiness requires both wanting what one possesses and possessing what one desires, with both pathways accounting for unique variance.

phenomenon: representation precision and information pooling under attention withdrawal
claim: withdrawn attention degrades local feature representation but preserves abstract statistical summaries through feature pooling mechanisms.

phenomenon: motivational bases of self-sacrificial intergroup behavior
claim: cooperative intergroup behavior is primarily motivated by altruistic commitment to in-group welfare rather than aggressive harming of out-groups.

phenomenon: internal averaging capacity and collective judgment
claim: individuals can partially replicate wisdom-of-crowds accuracy improvements by internally averaging their own beliefs across different generating processes.

phenomenon: differential memory consolidation for emotional scene components
claim: sleep selectively preserves emotionally salient focal objects while allowing background details to fade, whereas wake produces uniform decay across all components.

phenomenon: asymmetric valuation of future versus past equivalent events
claim: identical future events are valued more highly than past events, a temporal value asymmetry driven by greater affective engagement with future possibilities.

phenomenon: influence of physical purity salience on moral judgment severity
claim: activation of physical cleanliness concepts reduces severity of moral judgments, revealing moral cognition's dependence on embodied purity intuitions.

phenomenon: grounding abstract concepts in bodily sensation
claim: experience of physical weight grounds the abstract concept of importance, causing heavier objects to increase perceived value and cognitive elaboration.

phenomenon: neural asymmetry and conscious body size perception
claim: rightward asymmetries in cortical body representation for right-handed individuals correlate with perceived elongation of the dominant arm and hand.

phenomenon: unconscious operation of moral principles in judgment formation
claim: the principle of double effect guides moral judgments consistently across diverse populations despite participants' inability to articulate this reasoning.

phenomenon: side-effect valence influencing knowledge attribution
claim: agents are judged to know less about morally positive side-effects than negative ones, extending valence effects from intentionality to epistemic attribution.
